senses.definition: give (an infant or animal) a specified name
examples: they called their daughter Hannah

senses.definition: have a specified name
examples: she is called Eva
a 1942 mystery called Time To Kill

senses.definition: address or refer to (someone) by a specified name, title, endearment, or term of abuse
examples: please call me Lucy
some call him a genius

senses.definition: refer to, consider, or describe (someone or something) as being
examples: he's the only person I would call a friend

senses.definition: cry out (a word or words)
examples: he heard an insistent voice calling his name
Meredith was already calling out a greeting

senses.definition: cry out to (someone) in order to summon them or attract their attention
examples: she heard Terry calling her
: I distinctly heard you call

senses.definition: make its characteristic cry
examples: overhead, a skylark called

senses.definition: shout out or chant (the steps and figures) to people performing a square dance or country dance.
make (a particular bid) during the auction
examples: her partner called 6♠

senses.definition: claim (a privilege) for oneself, typically by shouting out a particular word or set phrase
examples: I call first dibs on the bathroom

senses.definition: contact or attempt to contact (a person or number) by phone
examples: could I call you back?
at the first sign of heart attack symptoms call 911 immediately

senses.definition: use a phone to summon (someone or something, especially an emergency service or a taxi)
examples: her husband called an ambulance

senses.definition: order or request the attendance of
examples: representatives of all three teams have been called to appear before the stewards
I got a letter calling me for an interview

senses.definition: bring (a witness) into court to give evidence
examples: four expert witnesses were called

senses.definition: draw critical attention to someone's unacceptable actions or behavior
examples: she called him on his claim that the media were doing a bad job of covering the economy

senses.definition: cause (someone) to have a strong urge to choose a particular career or way of life
examples: he was called to the priesthood
I think teachers, really good teachers, are called to teach

senses.definition: announce or decide that (an event, especially a meeting, strike, or election) is to happen
examples: there appeared to be no alternative but to call a general election
the Allied forces called a ceasefire
she intends to call a meeting of the committee early next week

senses.definition: pay a brief visit
examples: he had promised Celia he would call in at the clinic
he called around last night looking for you

senses.definition: pronounce (a ball, stroke, or other action) to be the thing specified
examples: the linesman called the ball wide
senses.semanticHint: (of an umpire or other official in a game)

senses.definition: predict the result of (a future event, especially an election or a vote)
examples: in the Northeast, the race remains too close to call
few pundits risked calling the election for either Bush or Kerry

senses.definition: guess (the outcome) of tossing a coin
examples: Burnley called heads and won the toss
: “You call,” he said. “Heads or tails?”

senses.definition: cause (a subroutine) to be executed
examples: one subroutine may call another subroutine (or itself)

senses.definition: a cry made as a summons or to attract someone's attention
examples: a nearby fisherman heard their calls for help
in response to the call, a figure appeared

senses.definition: a series of notes sounded on a brass instrument as a signal to do something
examples: a bugle call to rise at 5:30

senses.definition: a direction in a square dance given by the caller.
a bid, response, or double
examples: the alternative call of 2♠ would be quite unsound

senses.definition: the characteristic cry of a bird or other animal
examples: it is best distinguished by its call, a loud “pwit”

senses.definition: a device used to imitate the cry of a particular bird or other animal
examples: turkeys in the wild don't sound like most turkey calls
he bought a duck call at the store
most hunters I know have at least one call tucked away in a pocket

senses.definition: an instance of speaking to someone on the phone or attempting to contact someone by phone
examples: I'll give you a call at around five
he stopped returning her calls
a ten-minute call to the emergency services

senses.definition: a brief social visit
examples: we paid a call on Howard

senses.definition: a visit or journey made by a doctor or other professional in response to a request for help, especially in an emergency situation
examples: the ambulance is out on a call
the district nurse used to make her calls on a bicycle

senses.definition: an appeal or demand for something to happen or be done
examples: the call for action was welcomed
a call to all sides to remain calm and refrain from violence
there are more and more calls on his time

senses.definition: demand or need for (goods or services)
examples: there was little call for work as sophisticated as his

senses.definition: an order or request for someone to be present
examples: he was delighted that so many former players had heeded the call to attend the conference

senses.definition: a vocation
examples: feeling the call to ministry, I started looking for a Bible college

senses.definition: a powerful force of attraction
examples: hikers can't resist the call of the Sierras

senses.definition: a decision or ruling made by an umpire or other official, traditionally conveyed by a shout, that the ball has gone out of play or that a rule has been breached
examples: he was visibly irritated with the umpire's calls

senses.definition: a decision, judgement, or prediction
examples: personally, I'm all in favor, but it's your call
that entrepreneurial instinct may account for his ability to make tough calls when profits are at stake
the two old foes are so evenly matched that it's anyone's call

senses.definition: a command to execute a subroutine
examples: parameter values may be changed by calls to a special purpose input specification subroutine

senses.definition: a demand for payment of lent or unpaid capital.
denoting or made with relatively expensive brands of liquor which customers request by name.
examples: $6 call liquor drinks

notes.content: late Old English ceallian, from Old Norse kalla ‘summon loudly’.

items.definition: used to express criticism of a person's decision or suggestion
examples: not taking the deal would have been a bad call

items.definition: cause people to notice
examples: he is seeking to call attention to himself by his crimes

items.definition: make a phone call reversing the charges.
what is required of a person professionally or in relation to their personal or moral obligations
examples: Eric goes well above the call of duty and puts himself out for people
she went above and beyond the call of duty by helping me numerous times over this trip
many of those who have answered the call of duty will spend this Christmas season far from home

items.definition: cause one to think of someone or something, especially through similarity
examples: the still lifes call to mind certain of Cézanne's works
: I cannot call to mind where I have seen you
items.phrase: call someone or something to mind

items.definition: ask those present at a meeting to be silent so that business may proceed
examples: Randy McGill called the large gathering to order
items.phrase: call someone or something to order

items.definition: used as a dismissive way of saying that someone has not been successful in an audition or job application
examples: we got a lot of responses like, Well our schedule is full but if we have an open date, we will be back in touch … don't call us, we'll call you
items.phrase: don't call us, we'll call you

items.definition: used to express approval of a person's decision or suggestion
examples: So you asked her to leave? Good call

items.definition: (of a person) able to be contacted in order to provide a professional service if necessary, but not formally on duty
examples: our technicians are on call around the clock

items.definition: used to describe something that one can genuinely feel belongs to one
examples: I had not an item to call my own

items.definition: near enough to be summoned by calling
examples: she moved into the guest room, within call of her father's room

items.definition: cause or require something to start working so that one can make use of it
examples: our active participation as spectators is called into play

items.definition: cause or provoke someone or something to appear or occur
examples: nothing called down the wrath of Nemesis quicker
he called down Clarence Drum about being so high and mighty

items.definition: make something necessary
examples: desperate times call for desperate measures
the report calls for an audit of endangered species
I'll call for you around seven
the forecast is calling for more rain
they're calling for temperatures in the 80s for the rest of the week

items.definition: cause a specified response
examples: few things call forth more compassion

items.definition: enlist someone's aid or services
examples: you can either do the work yourself or call in a local builder to help you
the bank would call in loans and foreign donations

items.definition: cancel an event or agreement
examples: they held a ballot on whether to call off industrial action
Gunda pleaded with him to call the dog off

items.definition: pay a visit to someone
examples: he's planning to call on Katherine today
we are able to call on academic staff with a wide variety of expertise
he called on the government to hold a plebiscite

items.definition: summon someone to deal with an emergency or provide a service
examples: the police were constantly called out for “emergencies” by the paranoid diva
people were calling him out for his negative comments
Dan had called her out on a couple of contradictions in her story

items.definition: read out a list of names to determine those present
examples: a gentleman proceeded to call over the names of the jury

items.definition: summon someone to serve in the armed forces
examples: they have called up more than 20,000 reservists
he was called up from Columbus to finish the season with the Yankees
I have a list of people to call up in the morning
the special effects that called up the Mars landscape were impressive
icons that allow you to call up a graphic
